question: I was fired. Does the company have to delete my photo and fingerprint from the time clock and turnstile?
gold_answer: Not automatically. Under Article 17 GDPR, personal data must be erased when no longer necessary, but employers may retain certain data to comply with legal obligations or defend against legal claims. Biometric data should be minimized and retention must be justified and proportionate.

This right to erasure applies unless retention is required by Union or Member State law or for the establishment, exercise, or defence of legal claims. The controller must also notify recipients of the erasure unless impossible or disproportionate. See Article 17(1) and Article 19.', 'full_answer': "Under Article 17(1), you have the right to obtain erasure of your personal data, such as photo and fingerprint, from the company's systems without undue delay when the data are no longer necessary for the purposes for which they were collected (e.g., after termination of employment). The company, as controller, is obligated to erase these data unless there is a legal obligation to retain them or they are needed for legal claims. Article 19 further requires the controller to communicate the erasure to any recipients of the data, unless this is impossible or involves disproportionate effort. There is no direct cost to you for exercising this right. However, Member State law or collective agreements may provide for specific retention periods or exceptions, especially in the employment context (Article 88).", 'key_snippet': 'The data subject shall have the right to obtain from the controller the erasure of personal data concerning him or her without undue delay ... where ... the personal data are no longer necessary in relation to the purposes for which they were collected or otherwise processed;', 'chunks': ['chapter_iii_article_17', 'chapter_iii_article_19'], 'percentage': ['80', '20']}
